All reports
Fenn & Hart
PITCHPRO

Strategic Alignment Brief

One source of truth, and the moves that close the gap between it and the market.

Prepared by Fenn & Hart
June 2026

Part 1. Executive Summary and the Unified Position

Bottom line. PitchPro is the roofing-native platform for the $2-10M contractor who runs the whole business from one place, lead to QuickBooks, with automation built to all but run the job tracking itself. The job this brief sets is to make the market see that platform, fix the few public surfaces that contradict it today, and decide the handful of calls the evidence has teed up but not closed.

The position

Donnie O’Zane built PitchPro on a plain belief: a roofer should run the whole business from one platform, “everything from a lead to when you export it to QuickBooks,” with so little manual upkeep that the system “runs with very little human interaction.”1 That is the destination. PitchPro is the roofing-native operating system for the underserved $2-10M contractor, the operator who uses lots of software, is tech-savvy enough to run a desktop tool, and works mostly through per-job subcontractors the quoting and profitability tools already account for.2 It wins on three load-bearing advantages: roofing-native depth across the full lead-to-QuickBooks workflow, automation built to all but run itself, and predictable headcount-based pricing for a segment the incumbents nickel-and-dime. Eighty percent of contractors still run fragmented, disconnected systems3, and 84% manually re-enter data at a cost of three to five hours a week per user4. PitchPro turns that chaos into one platform so a working roofer feels relief instead of overhead.

The moat, or the moat opportunity

PitchPro has no proven durable moat today. Consolidation and flat pricing are real near-term advantages, and both are replicable: Roofr already markets itself verbatim as “all-in-one” across at least five live pages and serves 12,000+ companies5, and Roofr already prices flat with no per-seat charge plus a permanent $0 tier6. A competitor can match either move.

The strongest candidate to build a moat is a proprietary roofing pricing and job-profitability dataset, fed by the AI that ingests measurement PDFs and returns priced quotes7. If it reaches volume it would compound: better pricing and profitability recommendations attract more roofers, whose quotes make the recommendations sharper still, which a rival cannot copy by adding features. It is unproven today. PitchPro is pre-revenue with no customers and therefore no data flowing yet8, and the AI ingest is a claimed product feature, not evidence that pricing and profitability data is being captured and learned from. The test that decides it: reach a quote volume where PitchPro’s pricing and profitability recommendations are measurably more accurate than a cold competitor’s, and secure at least one supplier or data relationship a new entrant could not assemble off the shelf. Instrument that capture from the first customer; if the asset compounds, this becomes the moat to lead on. Roofr, not PitchPro, holds the live structural advantage in this category today, through ABC Supply equity and the SRS Distribution price feed across 430+ locations9.

How this brief is built

This brief builds one source of truth from three lenses. Where the lenses diverge, it names the gap and the move that closes it.

  • Internal View - what leadership believes, from the founder interview. Weighted most heavily; it is the anchor, and it stays open to correction.
  • Customer-Facing View - what the PitchPro website and public materials say today. A surface to bring into line with the position.
  • Market Perception - how roofing contractors and the market see the category now, from competitor copy, reviews, and category research. Where it diverges from the Internal View, that tells the messaging which perception it must move.

Leadership Perspectives on PitchPro

PitchPro is founder-led, so the source of truth is one operator describing several true layers of the same platform from the seat he sits in.

  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder, on the product. One platform covering everything from a lead to QuickBooks export, with automation so it runs with little human interaction.1
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder, on the customer. A roofer running lots of software, tech-savvy enough to operate the desktop tool, working mostly through per-job subcontractors.2
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder, on the economic case. Flat $399 a month for up to 20 employees, against incumbent spend of at least $400 and over $1,000 for a full stack.10
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder, on the durable advantage. “You could never stop somebody from copying you, but the fact that we have all the necessary tools in one place.”11
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder, on the ambition. Many happy customers and recurring revenue, a product that feels like “a trusted buddy that just simplified their life.”12

The job is to close the gap

  • The market sees four to six “all-in-one” roofing platforms competing on the same phrase13, where leadership sees one platform built to run itself for the $2-10M roofer.
  • The market sees Roofr free to start and flat-priced6, where leadership sees a predictable single bill with no per-seat tax and no add-on surprises.
  • The market sees high-intent buyers searching “roofing CRM” and finding competitors14, where leadership has assumed those buyers are not searching at all.

The perception lags the truth on every line. Every move in this brief closes that distance.

The proof already exists

No customer proof exists yet. PitchPro is pre-revenue with $0 revenue, no customers, no testimonials, and no case studies; the founder stated it plainly in the interview.8 The public site carries no testimonials, logo walls, or outcome statistics. State the case categorically until proof exists: the fragmentation it solves is documented across the category3 4, the cost gap it attacks is real15, and the competitor weaknesses it exploits are live and sourced16 17. What remains is to make the market see the platform the way leadership does, fix the public surfaces that contradict it before marketing the promise harder, and collect the first named outcomes from trial customers so the next version of this brief can lead with proof. Securing those case studies is a near-term decision for leadership, listed in the roadmap below.

The 5 alignment points at a glance

A scannable view of every alignment point so the whole set sits on one page before the detail. The Confidence column tells the team where the evidence settles the call and where it leaves a conversation to have.

# Alignment point How to close the gap Confidence Effort
1 The spine: one platform, built to run itself Lead every surface with roofing-native, runs-itself automation for the $2-10M roofer; pour engineering into clean QuickBooks, real mobile, and no add-on surprises 3/5 (Lean) 4/5
2 Fix the “only all-in-one” claim and the broken surfaces Replace “only all-in-one” with a defensible specific; take Stripe live, replace the placeholder demo, fill the empty pages 5/5 (Settled) 2/5
3 Win on predictable total cost, never cheapest Position one predictable bill, no per-seat tax, up to 20 people for $399; build a net-cost comparison as a sales tool 3/5 (Lean) 3/5
4 The moat opportunity: the roofing pricing data asset Name consolidation as a present wedge and the pricing/profitability data asset as the unproven moat opportunity; instrument it from day one 2/5 (Open) 4/5
5 Capture search demand, keep the push Run both: keep outbound to the passive majority, stand up content and a small paid-search test for buyers already searching 3/5 (Lean) 3/5

Confidence (evidence strength): 5 Settled

  • act now; 4 Strong lean
  • act and close the gap; 3 Lean
  • confirm before committing; 2 Open
  • needs more data; 1 Judgment call
  • leadership’s to make.

What you can act on, and what you need to decide

This brief creates the alignment the evidence supports and tees up the conversations the evidence leaves open.

What the evidence settles (act on these). Replace “the only all-in-one” everywhere with a defensible specific, and repair the public surfaces before any campaign ships. Roofr uses “all-in-one” verbatim across at least five live pages5, Housecall Pro uses it sitewide18, and four of the top six roofing platforms use the phrase13, so the superlative is disprovable the moment a buyer checks. The lever is PitchPro’s own editable copy, the Stripe checkout still in test mode19, and the placeholder demo page20. Make these fixes now.

What you need to discuss and decide.

The spine of the brand. The evidence leans toward leading with roofing-native, runs-itself automation, because that frame sits where the biggest competitor gaps and the strongest customer cravings overlap: Roofr has no native mobile app and thin QuickBooks sync16 17, JobNimbus QuickBooks sync breaks for customers21 22, and roofers say they crave one integrated system23 24. The “runs itself” half cannot be demonstrated yet against a placeholder demo. Discuss: if the auto-advancing whiteboard and 15-second quote cannot be shown live within the next sprint, lead with them anyway, or let speed-to-quote carry the headline until the full automation is provable? Trial-user message tests and a working demo would settle it.

Pricing. The evidence leans toward winning on predictable total cost of ownership, never on being cheapest, because incumbent stacks genuinely run $800-$2,600 a month15 and add-on resentment is real across competitors25, while Roofr is already flat with a $0 tier6 and PitchPro’s $399 floor sits at the bottom of the stack it claims to replace26. Discuss: for a typical $2-10M roofer pulling a given number of measurement reports a month, does flat $399 actually beat Roofr’s $349 plus per-report fees plus add-ons, and do you know your target customer’s real report volume? A net-cost model across low, medium, and high report usage would settle it.

The moat. The evidence does not decide this. Consolidation is a wedge any funded rival can copy and Roofr already has5, and the pricing/profitability data asset is structurally plausible but entirely unproven at zero customers8. Discuss: is PitchPro instrumenting from day one to capture and learn from every quote’s pricing and realized profitability, so that asset compounds rather than accumulating dead records? Only post-launch instrumented data plus a defensible supplier or data relationship would settle it.

Channel. The evidence leans toward running both push and capture, because “roofing CRM” and “roofing software” are active search categories with competitors ranking and running paid search14, while PitchPro’s own blog sits empty27. Discuss: before committing the budget split, will you run a small paid-search test against an outbound test and let cost-per-activated-trial decide the mix? Keyword volume data and that head-to-head test would settle it.

The cost of action/inaction

One consolidated framing covering all the moves in the brief. This is the only place stakes are framed.

The opportunity in closing the distance

  • A defensible headline lets PitchPro launch against a funded incumbent without forfeiting credibility on day one, where the current superlative collapses the moment a buyer checks Roofr’s homepage5.
  • The $2-10M segment is documented as underserved, sitting between cheap general tools and premium platforms that charge $1,200-$5,000+ a month28, and a clear position claims it.
  • Out-executing on the operational backbone Roofr has not finished, native mobile and true two-way QuickBooks16 17, turns competitor gaps into PitchPro’s reason to switch.
  • Capturing the high-intent buyers already searching “roofing CRM”14 converts demand PitchPro currently captures none of, from an asset (the blog) that is free to build.
  • Instrumenting the pricing and profitability data from the first customer starts the one asset that could become a moat, while it costs almost nothing to begin.

The cost of the action items

  • PitchPro surrenders the boldest headline the founder reaches for, the “only all-in-one” superlative, and accepts a narrower claim it can actually hold.
  • Launch energy diverts into unglamorous site repair, taking Stripe live, replacing the demo, filling empty pages19 20 27, before any campaign can run.
  • Scarce pre-revenue engineering goes into QuickBooks-clean plumbing and a real mobile experience rather than flashy new modules, and the runs-itself promise cannot be demonstrated until that work ships.
  • Giving up the “cheapest” claim and forgoing a free tier cedes the smallest solo operators below the $2-10M band and pure price-shoppers to Roofr’s $0 Starter6.
  • Splitting thin pre-revenue budget across outbound and search starts search visibility from zero against incumbents who already rank14, with slow compounding returns.

Part 2. The 5 Alignment Points in Detail

PitchPro is the roofing-native operating system for the underserved $2-10M contractor. It turns the lead-to-QuickBooks chaos of five disconnected tools into one platform built to all but run itself, with predictable headcount-based pricing in place of per-seat-plus-add-on stacks, so a working roofer finally feels relief instead of overhead. The five points below get the team and the market to that whole.

Alignment Point 1. The Spine: One Platform, Built to Run Itself

Bottom line. The defensible spine is roofing-native automation across the full lead-to-QuickBooks workflow for the $2-10M roofer, the one frame where the biggest competitor gaps and the strongest customer cravings overlap. Lead the brand here and build the unglamorous backbone rivals fail at. The evidence leans this way; confirm it with a live demo and trial-user tests.
  1. What the market sees. Roofers say one integrated system is the reason they stay and the thing they cannot find. “I stay because I haven’t found anything else that integrates project management, crm and a calendar in one software.”23 “I like that AccuLynx keeps all jobs, leads, documents, photos, notes, and communication inside one system. It eliminates the chaos of using multiple apps.”24 The incumbents leave the backbone unfinished: Roofr has no native mobile app and lists Production Scheduling as a 2026 focus16, Roofr’s QuickBooks integration is “limited” with no true two-way sync and thin labor costing17, and JobNimbus customers report QuickBooks sync that breaks their books21 22. The founder’s own framing names the spine directly: one platform, “everything from a lead to when you export it to QuickBooks,” running with “very little human interaction.”1
  2. The move. Lead every surface with roofing-native, runs-itself automation for the $2-10M roofer, the auto-advancing whiteboard and the 15-second AI quote, and pour scarce engineering into the operational backbone the incumbents fail at: clean two-way QuickBooks, a real mobile experience, and no add-on surprises. The well-evidenced half is the operational backbone, which is provable competitor whitespace. The “runs itself” half is the promise PitchPro is building toward and cannot yet demonstrate against a placeholder demo20.
  3. The question to discuss. If the auto-advancing whiteboard and 15-second quote cannot be demonstrated live within the next sprint, are you willing to lead with them anyway, or should speed-to-quote carry the headline until the full automation is provable?
  4. The bet: that “built to run itself” plus a finished operational backbone is the one position a funded incumbent has left open, and that winning the boring parts (QuickBooks, mobile) beats shipping flashy modules. The tradeoff: PitchPro stops saying “the only all-in-one,” bets scarce pre-revenue engineering on plumbing rather than features, and disappoints buyers who want a proven, demonstrable system today, since the demo and automation are not yet live. Action. Ship a working demo of the 15-second quote and auto-advancing whiteboard, then run trial-user message tests to decide automation-as-spine versus speed-as-hook. [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 4
  5. Confidence. 3/5. What would raise it: a working demo of the 15-second quote and auto-advancing whiteboard plus trial-user tests measuring which message drives sign-up and activation.

Alignment Point 2. Fix the “Only All-in-One” Claim and the Broken Surfaces

Bottom line. “The only all-in-one” is false the moment a buyer checks a competitor, and PitchPro controls the fix by editing its own copy and repairing its own broken surfaces. This is a do-it-now correction PitchPro fully owns. The evidence settles this.
  1. What the market sees. Roofr’s homepage reads verbatim “All-in-one roofing software with CRM, instant estimates, proposals, measurements, payments & material ordering,” and the phrase repeats across its homepage video, its Series B press release, and its small-business page, at least five live locations.5 Housecall Pro positions sitewide as “The #1 all-in-one solution for your home services business.”18 Four of the top six roofing platforms use “all-in-one” in primary positioning.13 On PitchPro’s own site, the checkout still carries a visible “Test mode” warning19, and the “Watch a demo” button resolves to a placeholder page with a broken image and “Title / Body” copy20.
  2. The move. Replace “the only all-in-one” everywhere with a defensible specific: the roofing-native platform built to run itself for the $2-10M contractor. Before any campaign ships, take Stripe out of test mode, replace the placeholder demo with a real walkthrough, and fill the empty About and blog pages27. The underlying insight survives: consolidation relieves genuine fragmentation pain3 4. The error is competitive-awareness, and the cost of leaving it is forfeiting credibility against a funded rival on day one.
  3. The bet: that a narrower, true claim launched against working surfaces beats a category-king superlative launched against a broken demo. The tradeoff: PitchPro gives up the simplest, boldest headline the founder loves and the dopamine of a superlative, and diverts launch energy into site repair before it can market, delaying outreach. Action. Rewrite the “only all-in-one” copy to the defensible specific and take the checkout, demo, and empty pages live. [Decide and Pivot] Effort: 2
  4. Confidence. 5/5. What would raise it: nothing further is needed to act; a verbatim competitor page cannot be un-falsified.

Alignment Point 3. Win on Predictable Total Cost, Never Cheapest

Bottom line. Flat headcount-based pricing is a real wedge on contested ground, so win on predictable total cost of ownership for the $2-10M segment and never claim “cheapest,” because Roofr is already flat and cheaper at entry. The evidence leans this way; confirm it with your customer’s real report volume.
  1. What the market sees. Incumbent per-user stacks punish growth and breed resentment. AccuLynx Essential starts at $250 a month with per-user Pro and Elite tiers, and a JobNimbus ten-person team can top $1,200 a month15. Roofers say it plainly: “Everything is an add-on, and you have to commit to the whole portion of that add-on in regard to price… when you start to crest into the thousand-dollar range, the bang for your buck is lessened.”29 “The price is a bit high and if you want to utilize some additional features you have to pay substantially more.”30 Roofr complicates the simple savings story: it does not charge per seat and runs a permanent $0 Starter tier6, and PitchPro’s $399 floor sits at the bottom of the $400-$900 stack it claims to replace26.
  2. The move. Position pricing as one predictable bill, no per-seat tax, no add-on surprises, up to 20 people for $399, aimed at the $2-10M roofer being nickel-and-dimed by add-on stacks. Build a net-cost comparison that nets in measurement-report fees and add-ons rather than quoting a sticker price, and treat it as a sales-enablement tool, not a headline, until the target customer’s report volume is known. The durable core is predictability and no add-on surprises, which holds regardless of the head-to-head entry-price outcome.
  3. The question to discuss. For a typical $2-10M roofer pulling a given number of measurement reports a month, does PitchPro’s flat $399 actually beat Roofr’s $349 plus per-report fees plus add-ons, and do you know your target customer’s real report volume?
  4. The bet: that predictability and no add-on surprises win the $2-10M operator who is tired of being nickel-and-dimed, even when a rival is cheaper to start. The tradeoff: PitchPro gives up the punchy “cheapest” claim and forgoes a free tier to match Roofr’s $0 funnel, accepting that price-shoppers and the smallest solo operators below the band choose Roofr’s free Starter. Action. Build the net-cost comparison model across low, medium, and high report usage and ship it as a sales tool. [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 3
  5. Confidence. 3/5. What would raise it: a net-cost model for a representative $2-10M roofer, PitchPro flat $399 against Roofr Scale $349 plus reports times volume plus needed add-ons, across low, medium, and high measurement-report usage.

Alignment Point 4. The Moat Opportunity: The Roofing Pricing Data Asset

Bottom line. Consolidation is the founder’s honest durable-advantage claim, and it is a wedge any funded rival can copy, which Roofr already has. The only structurally shaped moat candidate is a not-yet-built roofing pricing and profitability data asset. Name it as an opportunity, never as a proven moat. The evidence does not decide this; it is a call for the team.
  1. What the market sees. The founder is candid about copyability: “you could never stop somebody from copying you, but the fact that we have all the necessary tools in one place.”11 The market bears him out that consolidation is copyable: Roofr markets all-in-one verbatim, ships the full quote-to-cash workflow, and serves 12,000+ companies on Series B money5 31. Roofr holds the live structural advantage through ABC Supply equity and a real-time SRS Distribution price feed across 430+ locations9, an asset PitchPro cannot match without comparable distributor relationships. PitchPro is pre-revenue with no customers and no data flowing yet8.
  2. Where the evidence points. The one candidate with the shape of a moat is a proprietary roofing quote, pricing, and job-profitability dataset, fed by the AI that ingests measurement PDFs and returns priced quotes7. If it reaches volume it compounds and corners: recommendations get more accurate the more quotes flow through, which rivals cannot copy by adding features. What is missing is decisive: the AI ingest is a claimed feature, not evidence that pricing and profitability data is being captured and learned from, and there is no volume at zero customers8. The honest position is consolidation as a present speed-to-value wedge and the data asset as a moat opportunity with explicit prove-it conditions.
  3. The question to discuss. Is PitchPro instrumenting from day one to capture and learn from every quote’s pricing and realized profitability, so that asset compounds rather than just accumulating dead records?
  4. The bet: that naming the moat honestly buys credibility with any sophisticated buyer or acquirer, and that instrumenting early starts an asset that could become uncopyable. The tradeoff: PitchPro and the founder give up the comfortable story that “all the tools in one place” is itself the durable advantage, and accept the uncomfortable admission that today it has no proven moat while a rival holds the live one. Action. Hold the data-instrumentation decision with engineering and commit to capturing every quote’s pricing and realized profitability from the first customer, then set the volume and accuracy targets that would prove the asset. [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 4
  5. Confidence. 2/5. What would raise it: a quote and job volume where PitchPro’s pricing and profitability recommendations are measurably more accurate than a cold competitor’s, plus at least one defensible supplier or data relationship a new entrant could not assemble off the shelf.

Alignment Point 5. Capture Search Demand, Keep the Push

Bottom line. Many small roofers are passive and must be pushed to, and a measurable number are actively searching “roofing software,” so ceding search hands high-intent buyers to incumbents for free. Run both, and fill the empty blog that is bleeding traffic now. The evidence leans this way; confirm the mix with a test.
  1. What the market sees. “Roofing CRM” and “roofing software” are active search categories: at least seven competing “best roofing CRM” roundup pages rank, agencies treat the terms as high-intent buyer searches, and vendors run paid search14. Exact keyword volume was not obtainable through public sources, so the read is directional. PitchPro captures none of that demand, because its blog returns an empty page27.
  2. The move. Run both push and capture: keep the outbound to the passive majority and the 15-day trial as the low-friction conversion offer, and stand up a capture layer on PitchPro’s own site, filling the empty blog with roofing-specific content and running a small paid-search test on “roofing CRM” and “roofing software.” The founder’s instinct that small roofers are passive is half-right; the both-and adds a capture layer for the buyers who are searching without retracting the push thesis.
  3. The question to discuss. Before committing the budget split, will you run a small paid-search test against an outbound test and let cost-per-activated-trial decide the mix, rather than assuming push-only?
  4. The bet: that high-intent search demand is real enough to capture cheaply while the push motion reaches the passive majority. The tradeoff: PitchPro splits thin pre-revenue budget and founder attention across two motions instead of going all-in on outbound, and starts search visibility from zero against incumbents who already rank, accepting slow compounding returns. Action. Fill the blog with roofing-specific content and run a small paid-search test against an outbound test, comparing cost-per-activated-trial. [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 3
  5. Confidence. 3/5. What would raise it: actual keyword volume and CPC data for “roofing CRM” and “roofing software,” plus a small paid-search test versus an outbound test comparing cost-per-activated-trial.

Part 3. Strategic Pre-Mortem: The View from Failure

The ways this repositioning fails, and how to prevent each.

Failure Mode 1. The headline ships before the surfaces work

Risk. PitchPro launches a campaign on the new “built to run itself” position while the checkout still reads “Test mode”19 and the “Watch a demo” button lands on a placeholder20. A high-intent buyer arrives, hits a broken demo, and leaves, and the launch spends its scarcest resource, first impressions, on surfaces that contradict the promise. Prevention. Treat the surface repair as the gate on any outreach. Take Stripe live, replace the demo with a real walkthrough, and fill the empty pages before a single dollar of demand-generation runs.

Failure Mode 2. “Runs itself” is claimed before it can be shown

Risk. The brand leads on automation that cannot be demonstrated against a placeholder demo20, with no customers to vouch for it8. A buyer asks to see the auto-advancing whiteboard run, there is nothing to show, and the headline reads as vaporware against a funded incumbent that ships a working product. Prevention. Ship a working demo of the 15-second quote and the auto-advancing whiteboard before leading on “runs itself.” Until it can be shown, let speed-to-quote carry the headline and treat automation as the promise PitchPro is building toward.

Failure Mode 3. Consolidation gets dressed up as the moat

Risk. The deck and the brand claim that “all the tools in one place” is the durable advantage. A sophisticated buyer or acquirer runs diligence, sees Roofr already replicates consolidation and holds the live supply-chain advantage5 9, and the moat claim collapses, taking credibility with it. Prevention. Name consolidation as a present wedge and the pricing/profitability data asset as the unproven moat opportunity. Instrument the data capture from the first customer so the real candidate starts compounding.

Failure Mode 4. A move waits on an outside yes that does not come

Risk. Several moves depend on approvals PitchPro does not control: a supplier or data relationship to anchor the moat candidate, named-customer releases to publish the first case studies, and any future carrier approval for the insurance report. If the strategy is built to wait on those, the launch stalls on a yes that may never arrive. Prevention (mitigation). Start the supplier and data-relationship conversations early and in parallel with the build, and secure signed testimonial-release language in the first trial-customer agreements so case studies are publishable the moment outcomes exist. Prevention (fallback). Ship the launch on the surfaces and proof PitchPro fully controls, the operational backbone, the predictable-cost position, and the captured search demand, so the pivot is never blocked on a single external approval.

Part 4. Implementation Roadmap

Three phases of concrete, executable work. Every action tags a confidence tier and an effort score. No calendar dates.

Decision and ownership matrix

Decision Owner (function) Precondition / Gate Ship-or-kill criterion
Replace “only all-in-one” with the defensible specific Founder / Marketing None; copy is fully controlled Ship once new copy is live across all surfaces
Take checkout, demo, and empty pages live Founder / Product None; surfaces are fully controlled Ship once Stripe is in main mode and the demo runs
Lead the brand on “built to run itself” Founder Working demo of the 15-second quote and whiteboard Go if the demo runs and trial tests favor automation; otherwise lead with speed-to-quote
Claim a pricing total-cost advantage Founder / Marketing Net-cost model across report-volume bands Go if flat $399 beats Roofr net cost at the target volume; otherwise hold to “predictable, no surprises”
Lead on the data-asset moat Founder / Engineering Instrumented capture plus measurable accuracy edge and a defensible supplier relationship (ties to Failure Mode 4) Go only once the asset measurably compounds; otherwise keep as opportunity
Commit a push/search budget split Founder / Marketing Paid-search test vs. outbound test on cost-per-activated-trial Allocate to whichever channel wins on cost-per-activated-trial

Phase 1. Alignment and cleanup

  • Rewrite the headline claim [Decide and Pivot] Effort: 2. Replace “the only all-in-one” everywhere with “the roofing-native platform built to run itself for the $2-10M contractor.”
  • Repair the public surfaces [Decide and Pivot] Effort: 2. Take Stripe out of test mode, replace the placeholder demo with a real walkthrough, and fill the empty About and blog pages.
  • Language guardrails [Decide and Pivot] Effort: 1. Swap “only all-in-one” for “roofing-native, built to run itself”; swap “cheapest” for “one predictable bill, no per-seat tax, no add-on surprises”; swap “the moat is all the tools in one place” for “consolidation is our present advantage; the data asset is the moat we are building.”

Phase 2. Asset development

  • Ship a working demo [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 4. Build a live walkthrough of the 15-second quote and the auto-advancing whiteboard so the runs-itself promise can be shown.
  • Build the net-cost comparison tool [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 3. Model PitchPro flat $399 against Roofr net cost across low, medium, and high measurement-report volume, for sales enablement.
  • Instrument the data asset [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 4. Capture every quote’s pricing and realized profitability from the first customer so the moat candidate compounds.
  • Stand up the content layer [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 3. Fill the blog with roofing-specific content targeting “roofing CRM” and “roofing software.”

Phase 3. Market penetration

  • Run the channel test [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 3. Run a small paid-search test against an outbound test and allocate budget by cost-per-activated-trial.
  • Collect the first proof [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 2. Secure testimonial-release language in trial-customer agreements and publish the first named outcomes as case studies.
  • Open the supplier and data conversations [Requires Next-Phase Scoping] Effort: 4. Pursue at least one defensible supplier or data relationship a new entrant could not assemble off the shelf.

Part 5. What Success Looks Like

Three horizons of directional outcomes. No metric targets.

First 90 days

  • Surfaces and headline aligned: The site says one true thing about who PitchPro is, the checkout takes real payment, and the demo runs. Count the public surfaces still carrying “only all-in-one” or placeholder content; the signal is that count reaching zero.

First year

  • A demonstrable spine and first proof: The runs-itself automation can be shown live, the first trial customers are activating, and the first named outcomes exist to quote. Count activated trial customers and published case studies as they accrue.

Steady state

  • A compounding data asset and a captured channel: Quote volume is feeding pricing and profitability recommendations that get sharper over time, and a defined share of new trials arrives from search the company captured itself. Count the quotes flowing through the pricing engine and the trials sourced from organic and paid search.

Appendix A. Rationale for Dropping “The Only All-in-One”

The move most likely to draw internal pushback is retiring the founder’s favorite headline. “There is no all-in-one software other than us”32 is the boldest, simplest claim PitchPro has, and giving it up feels like surrendering the high ground. The evidence makes the opposite case: keeping it is the high-risk move.

The claim is falsified the moment a buyer looks. Roofr self-describes as “all-in-one roofing software” verbatim across at least five live pages and serves 12,000+ companies5 31. Housecall Pro carries the same claim sitewide18, and four of the top six roofing platforms use the phrase13. A superlative that a thirty-second search disproves does not build credibility; it spends it. And it spends it against a funded incumbent while PitchPro’s own checkout reads “Test mode”19 and its demo is a placeholder20.

Consolidation relieves fragmentation pain the category documents: 80% of contractors run disconnected systems3 and 84% manually re-enter data at three to five hours a week4. The job is to keep that consolidation insight and sharpen it into a claim PitchPro can hold, the roofing-native platform built to run itself for the $2-10M contractor.

Addressing the likely objections

  • “All-in-one is what buyers search for; we lose discoverability.” Keep “all-in-one” in supporting copy and metadata where it earns search relevance; drop only the false superlative “the only.” The capture-demand work in Alignment Point 5 covers discoverability directly.
  • “A narrower claim sounds weaker.” A claim a buyer can verify is stronger than one they can disprove. “Built to run itself for the $2-10M roofer” is specific, differentiated, and aimed at a documented underserved segment28.
  • “Roofr is bigger; we cannot out-position them.” PitchPro does not have to. Roofr has live gaps, no native mobile and thin QuickBooks16 17, and the new position aims squarely at them.
  • “This delays our launch.” The surface repair is small effort and fully controlled. The delay is days of cleanup against the cost of launching a disprovable claim on a broken site.
  • “The founder believes the original claim.” The reframe sharpens his own words rather than overruling them; “all the tools in one place”11 becomes the literal spine of the brand.

Retiring one false word protects everything true about the position. The recommendation is to make the swap now, because it is the cheapest, highest-leverage move in the brief and the only one that needs no further evidence to act.

Appendix C. Interview Voices Reference

Every founder quote that fed a Part 2 synthesis, preserved verbatim and attributed.

Alignment Point 1: The Spine: One Platform, Built to Run Itself

  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “the bundling of different software packages in one place, and how it covers everything from a lead to when you export it to QuickBooks.”
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “it’s got very little human interaction to see where your jobs are at at all times.”

Alignment Point 2: Fix the “Only All-in-One” Claim and the Broken Surfaces

  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “There is no all-in-one software other than us. No?”
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “but they’re in three separate places.”

Alignment Point 3: Win on Predictable Total Cost, Never Cheapest

  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “they’re probably spending at least 400”
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “If they’re using a full software stack, then anywhere over $1,000 a month.”
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “it’s a month-by-month subscription for $399 up to 20 employees”

Alignment Point 4: The Moat Opportunity: The Roofing Pricing Data Asset

  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “you could never stop somebody from copying you, but the fact that we have all the necessary tools in one place so you don’t have to have four or five subscriptions and four or five different invoices every month.”
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “Because we do what they do plus more.”

Alignment Point 5: Capture Search Demand, Keep the Push

  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “I don’t know that they’re going to go looking for a solution, but I think if a solution was put in front of them, they would be interested.”
  • Donnie O’Zane, Founder: “There’s a 15-day trial. It can’t hurt. Let me check it out.”
